title: Introduction
content_preview: The introduction outlines the comparison of political and constitutional crises in Pakistan from 1947-1956 and post-2008, highlighting how they reflect structural weaknesses in governance.

title: 1947-1956: A State Without a stable
content_preview: This section examines the early crises in Pakistan, characterized by instability without stable governance.

title: a) Leadership Vacuum post independence
content_preview: Following independence, the death of Quaid-e-Azam in 1948 and Liaquat Ali Khan's assassination left Pakistan without strong leadership, resulting in four prime ministers in four years.

title: b) Constitutional Delay and Institutional paralysis
content_preview: Pakistan lacked a permanent constitution for nine years, relying on the 1935 Government of India Act, leading to institutional delays.

title: c) Arbitrary Executive Authority Undermined Parliament
content_preview: Governor-General Ghulam Muhammad dismissed Prime Minister Nazimuddin in 1953 despite parliamentary support, exemplifying arbitrary executive power.

title: d) Ethnic disunity due to forced provincial Mergers
content_preview: The 1955 One Unit scheme merged West Pakistan's provinces, sparking ethnic resentment in Balochistan and Sindh.

title: e) Judiciary Enabled Undemocratic Rule
content_preview: In the Tamizuddin Case of 1955, the court validated the dissolution of the Constituent Assembly by an unelected Governor-General.

title: Post 2008: A Democracy Captured by Power Struggles and Legal Battles
content_preview: This period features ongoing power struggles and legal manipulations undermining democracy.

title: (i) Civil-Military conflict behind democratic Facade
content_preview: The 2011 Memogate Scandal revealed civilian fears of military coups, as seen after Osama bin Laden's killing.

title: (ii) Judicial Overreach into political domain
content_preview: In 2017, Prime Minister Nawaz Sharif was disqualified over a minor iqama issue from the Panama leaks, viewed as politically motivated.

title: (iii) Weak and divided Coalition Governments
content_preview: The 2022 PDM alliance faced internal contradictions, hindering consistent governance and reforms.

title: iv) Politicized accountability undermines rule of law
content_preview: The NAB arrested opposition leaders disproportionately during PTI rule, criticized by HRCP.

title: V) Constitutional conflict between Judiciary and Executive
content_preview: The 2023 election deadlock in Punjab and KP highlighted failures to enforce constitutional timelines due to political interference in Article 224.

title: Broader Structural Weaknesses in Governance Across Both Eras
content_preview: Common issues persist across periods, revealing enduring governance flaws.

title: a) Lack of Institutional continuity
content_preview: Frequent changes in prime ministers (14 from 1947-2025) and weak parliamentary roots undermine sustainability.

title: b) Absence of Checks and Balances
content_preview: Leaders from Ghulam Muhammad to Imran Khan excessively used ordinances, bypassing checks.

title: (c) Overdependence on Unelected institutions
content_preview: The military's influence, via ISPR press conferences, shapes political narratives over civilian decisions.

title: d) Weak local Government system
content_preview: Grassroots democracy is neglected despite provisions; Punjab local elections were not held from 2016 to 2022.

title: 5- Critical Appraisal
content_preview: This section provides a critical evaluation of the crises and structural weaknesses discussed.

title: Conclusion
content_preview: The conclusion summarizes the reflections on structural weaknesses in Pakistani governance from both eras.
